title: Listening - Part 3: The Sleep Revolution
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with a neuroscientist, Abbi, and a sleep coach, Noah, about why society is suffering from a sleep crisis. For questions 15–20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best.

extracts.context: An interview discussing the 'glymphatic system,' the impact of blue light, and the economic cost of insomnia.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: We spend a third of our lives asleep, yet we often treat it as an optional luxury. Joining me are Dr. Abbi, a neuroscientist, and Noah, who works with corporate teams to improve their rest. Abbi, why is sleep so non-negotiable for the brain?

Abbi: [15] For a long time, we thought the brain just 'turned off.' Now we know it’s incredibly busy. During deep sleep, the 'glymphatic system' opens up—it’s like a biological dishwasher that flushes out metabolic waste. [/15] Without this 'rinse,' toxins like beta-amyloid build up, which are linked to Alzheimer’s. Sleep isn't just rest; it’s essential maintenance. 

Interviewer: Noah, you work with 'high-performers.' Is the 'I’ll sleep when I’m dead' mentality still common?

Noah: [16] Unfortunately, yes. In many corporate cultures, sleep deprivation is worn like a badge of honor. But a brain that has been awake for twenty hours is functionally equivalent to a brain that is legally drunk. [/16] You lose your ability to read social cues, your reaction time slows, and your creativity vanishes. Companies are starting to realize that a well-rested employee is much more valuable than a 'zombie' who works fifteen-hour days.

Interviewer: Abbi, what is the 'blue light' debate really about? Is it just the screens?

Abbi: [17] It’s about our 'circadian rhythm.' Blue light mimics the sun, which tells the brain to stop producing melatonin—the hormone that makes us sleepy. [/17] But it’s also about 'cognitive stimulation.' If you’re reading a stressful news story or a work email at midnight, your brain is in a state of high alert. It’s not just the light; it’s the content.

Interviewer: Noah, what are some practical 'sleep hygiene' tips for people who struggle to drift off?

Noah: [18] Temperature is the big one. Your core body temperature needs to drop by about one degree Celsius to initiate sleep. Most people keep their bedrooms too warm. [/18] Also, 'consistency' is king. Your brain has an internal clock that loves a routine. If you wake up at the same time every day—even on weekends—it becomes much easier to fall asleep at night.

Interviewer: Abbi, can we ever truly 'catch up' on sleep during the weekend?

Abbi: [19] Sadly, no. Sleep is not like a bank account where you can pay off a debt later. The damage done to your immune system and your memory during the week cannot be fully repaired by sleeping in on Sunday. [/19] It’s a biological necessity that must be met every twenty-four hours.

Interviewer: Final thoughts, Noah? Is the 'sleep revolution' actually happening?

Noah: [20] We are seeing a shift. People are starting to prioritize 'rest' as much as 'diet' and 'exercise.' We are moving away from a society that celebrates burnout and toward one that respects biology. [/20]

Interviewer: Abbi, Noah, thank you.

questions.question: Abbi explains that during deep sleep, the brain
options: A) becomes completely inactive to save energy.
B) uses a special system to clean out harmful waste.
C) produces the same amount of beta-amyloid as during the day.
D) creates new neurons to replace old ones.
questions.explanation: She refers to the 'glymphatic system' which acts like a 'dishwasher' to flush out metabolic waste.

questions.question: What comparison does Noah make regarding sleep deprivation?
options: A) It is like being under the influence of alcohol.
B) It is similar to having a serious physical injury.
C) It feels the same as running a marathon every day.
D) It is more dangerous than smoking cigarettes.
questions.explanation: He says a brain awake for 20 hours is 'functionally equivalent to a brain that is legally drunk'.

questions.question: According to Abbi, blue light affects sleep because it
options: A) physically damages the retina of the eye.
B) increases the production of melatonin too early.
C) tricks the brain into thinking it is still daytime.
D) makes it impossible to read stories on a screen.
questions.explanation: She states blue light mimics the sun and tells the brain to stop producing melatonin.

questions.question: Noah suggests that to fall asleep more easily, people should
options: A) keep their bedroom at a higher temperature.
B) exercise late at night to tire themselves out.
C) maintain a strictly consistent waking time.
D) sleep for at least ten hours every night.
questions.explanation: He advises waking up at the same time every day, even on weekends, to help the internal clock.

questions.question: What is Abbi's view on 'catching up' on sleep at the weekend?
options: A) It is an effective way to stay healthy for the following week.
B) It can repair the damage done to the immune system.
C) It is impossible to compensate for sleep lost during the week.
D) It is only possible if you sleep for more than twelve hours.
questions.explanation: She says sleep is not like a bank account and the damage from the week 'cannot be fully repaired'.

questions.question: Noah believes that society is now beginning to
options: A) value long working hours more than health.
B) understand that sleep is as important as physical exercise.
C) ban the use of smartphones after nine o'clock at night.
D) encourage people to sleep in their offices to save time.
questions.explanation: He notes a shift toward prioritizing rest as much as 'diet' and 'exercise'.
